title: Perimeter word problems
description: Practice solving real world word problems involving perimeter.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kiri is putting a frame around the greenhouse panel below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many centimeters of frame does Kiri need?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints: The frame goes *around* the outside of the panel.  So, we need to know the **perimeter**.

Perimeter can be found by adding the lengths of  all the sides.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD {190} + \greenD {131} + \redC {100} + \purpleC {95}  = {516 \,\text{cm}}$
Kiri needs $516\,\text{cm}$ of frame.

options.alt: A four-sided shape. Its sides are as follows: 190 centimeters up, 131 centimeters down diagonally to the right, 100 centimeters down, and 95 centimeters to the left.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nour is buying wood trim to put around the outside of her kitchen.  

[[☃ image 1]]

**How many meters of wood trim does Nour need?**  
 
[[☃ input-number 1]] $\text{m}$
hints: The wood trim goes *around* the outside of the kitchen.  So, we need to know the **perimeter**.

Perimeter can be found by adding the lengths of  all the sides.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD {5} + \greenD {3} + \redC {2} + \purpleC {3} + \tealD {3} + \goldD {6} = {22\,\text{m}}$
Nour needs $22\,\text{m}$ of wood trim.

options.alt: A six-sided figure in the shape of a rectangle with a smaller rectangle missing from the upper right corner is labeled Kitchen. The shape has lengths as follows: 5 meters down, six meters to the right, 3 meters up, 3 meters back to the left, 2 meters up, and 3 meters to the left.

assessment_items.question_content:
A walking path will go around the edges of a park as shown below.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How long, in kilometers, will the path be?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{km}$
hints: The path goes *around* the edges of the park.  So, we need to know the **perimeter**.

Perimeter can be found by adding the lengths of  all the sides.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD {5} + \greenD {1} + \redC {2} + \purpleC {3}  + \tealD{3} + \goldE{4}= {18\,\text{km}}$
The path will be $18\,\text{km}$ long.

options.alt: A six-sided shape is labeled Park. Its sides lengths are as follows: 5 kilometers to the right, 1 kilometer down, 2 kilometers to the left, 3 kilometers diagonally down to the right, 3 kilometers back to the left, and 4 kilometers diagonally up and to the left.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jules adds a border around his mirror.  The mirror is shaped like a triangle.  Each side is $35\,\text{cm}$ long.

**How long is the border?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text{cm}$
hints: The border goes *around* the mirror.  So, we need to know the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{35}+\greenD{35}+\greenD{35}$
The border is $105\,\text{cm}$ long.

assessment_items.question_content:
Alex jogs around the building below.  Each side of the building is $200\,\text{m}$ long.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How far does Alex jog?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text{m}$
hints: Alex jogs *around* the building.  So, we need to know the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{200}+\greenD{200}+\greenD{200}+\greenD{200}+\greenD{200}$

Since there are $\blueD{5}$ sides that are all $\greenD{200}$ meters long, we can also multiply.

$\text{Perimeter} = \blueD{5}\times\greenD{200}$

*Think:* $5 \times 2 \times 100$
Alex jogs $1{,}000\,\text{m}$.

options.alt: A five-sided shape known as a pentagon. Each of the five sides is the same length.

assessment_items.question_content:
Toni runs around her school's baseball diamond.  Each side is $27\,\text{m}$ long.  
*Note: A baseball diamond is square.*

**How far does Toni run?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{m}$
hints: Toni runs *around* the diamond.  So, we need to know the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{27}+\greenD{27}+\greenD{27}+\greenD{27}$
Toni runs $108\,\text{m}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Rob plans to build $3$ tennis courts in a local park. He will build a fence around the whole area.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How much fencing will Rob need?**  
*Each court is the same size.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints: The fence goes *around* the outside of the area, so we need to know the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the edges.  Only *outside* edges count for the perimeter.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \blueD{24} + \greenD{12} + \redD{24} + \purpleC{12} + \goldE{24} + \maroonC{12} + \kaBlue{12}$
Rob needs $120\text{ m}$ of fence.

options.alt: A rectangle made up of 3 rectangles that are the same size. The rectangle at the top is labeled Court 1. The two rectangles at the bottom are set side by side and are labeled Court 2 and Court 3. The width of Court 2 is labeled 12 meters and its height is labeled 24 meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Hana plans to build $3$ cricket courts in a local park.  She will build a fence around the whole area.

[[☃ image 1]]

**How much fencing will Hana need?**  
*Each court is the same size.*

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints: The fence goes *around* the outside of the area, so we need to know the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the edges.  Only *outside* edges count for the perimeter.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \blueD{30} + \greenD{11} + \redD{11} + \purpleC{11} + \goldE{30} + \maroonC{11} + \kaBlue{11}+11$
Hana needs $126\text{ m}$ of fence.

options.alt: A rectangle made up of 3 rectangles that are the same size set side by side. The rectangles are labeled Court 1, Court 2, and Court 3. The length of Court 1 is labeled 11 meters and its width is labeled 30 meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Arthur uses three $10\,\text{cm}$ by $15\,\text{cm}$ photos to make the following rectangle.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the perimeter of Arthur's rectangle?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints: The **perimeter** is the distance *around* an object.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the edges.  Only *outside* edges count for the perimeter.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \blueD{10} + \greenD{15} + \redD{15} + \purpleC{15} + \goldE{10} + \maroonC{15} + \kaBlue{15}+\purpleA{15}$
Arthur's rectangle has a perimeter of $110\,\text{cm}$.

options.alt: A rectangle made up of three rectangles that are the same size set side by side. Together, they are labeled Arthur's rectangle. The first rectangle has a length of 15 centimeters and a width of 10 centimeters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Bella uses three $24\,\text{cm}$ by $30\,\text{cm}$ photos to make the following rectangle.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the perimeter of Bella's rectangle?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints: The **perimeter** is the distance *around* an object.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the edges.  Only *outside* edges count for the perimeter.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \blueD{30} + \greenD{24} + \redD{24} + \purpleC{24} + \goldE{30} + \maroonC{24} + \kaBlue{24} + \purpleE{24}$
Bella's rectangle has a perimeter of $204\,\text{cm}$.

options.alt: A rectangle labeled Bella's rectangle that is made up of three rectangles of the same size. The first rectangle has a length of 24 centimeters and a height of 30 centimeters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Chris puts two layers of cake next to each other.  The layers each measure $20\,\text{cm}$ by $30\,\text{cm}$.  The figure below shows the cake.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the perimeter of the cake?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints: The **perimeter** is the distance *around* an object.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the edges.  Only *outside* edges count for the perimeter.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \blueD{30} + \greenD{20} + \redD{20} + \purpleC{30} + \goldE{20} + \maroonC{20}$
The perimeter is $140\,\text{cm}$.

options.alt: A rectangle made up of two rectangles of the same size. The first rectangle has a length of 20 centimeters and a height of 30 centimeters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Gus plans to install a hand rail around a skating rink.  The rink forms a $ 40\,\text{m}$ by $20 \,\text{m}$ rectangle.

**How many meters of hand rail does Gus need?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  $\text{m}$
hints: The hand rail goes around the skating rink, so we need to know the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \purpleC{20}+ \greenD{40}+\purpleC{20}+\greenD{40} $
Gus needs $120\,\text{m}$ of hand rail.

answers.message: This is only half way around the rink.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lulu has exactly enough fencing to go around her rectangular garden. She has $40$ meters of fencing. The garden is $8$ meters wide.

**What is the length of her garden?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints:
The fence goes *around* the outside of the area, so we know the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.

A rectangle has $4$ sides. 

[[☃ image 1]]

We know the width of the rectangle is $8\,\text{m}$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$8\text{ m} + \ell + 8\text{ m} +\ell = 40\text{ m}$

[[☃ image 1]]

$8\text{ m} + \greenD{12}\text{ m} + 8\text{ m} +\greenD{12}\text{ m} = 40\text{ m}$

[[☃ image 1]]

The missing side length is $\greenD{12}$ meters long.
Lulu's garden is $\greenD{12}$ meters long.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jun used $56\,\text{cm}$ of ribbon to go around the edge of a rectangular lid.  The lid was $13\,\text{cm}$ long.

**What is the width of the lid?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
The ribbon goes *around* the lid, so we know the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.

A rectangle has $4$ sides. 

[[☃ image 1]]

We know the length of the rectangle is $13\,\text{cm}$.  

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

$13\text{ cm} + w+ 13\text{ cm} + w= 56\text{ cm}$

[[☃ image 1]]

$13\text{ cm} + \greenD{15}\text{ cm} + 13\text{ cm} + \greenD{15}\text{ cm} = 56\text{ cm}$

[[☃ image 1]]

The missing side length is $\greenD{15}\,\text{cm}$ wide.
The lid is $\greenD{15}\,\text{cm}$ wide.

assessment_items.question_content:
Zayn puts garland around a square window. He uses $8$ meters of garland.

**What is the height of the window?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints:
The garland goes *around* the outside of the window, so we know the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.

[[☃ image 1]]

A square has $4$ equal sides. 

$8\,\text{m} = \greenE{{?}}+\greenE{{?}}+\greenE{{?}}+\greenE{{?}}$

Since there are $\purpleD{4}$ sides that are all $\greenE{?}\,\text{m}$ long, we can also multiply.

$8\,\text{m} = \purpleD{4}\times\greenE{{?}}$
The window has a height of $\greenE{2}$ meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jayden folded a $66 \text{ cm}$ strip of paper into $6$ equal parts to model a honeycomb cell. Each part was one side of the model.

**What is the length of one side of the model?**

[[☃ numeric-input 2]] $\text{cm}$
hints:
The sides go *around* the model.  So, we know the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.

[[☃ image 1]]

$66\,\text{cm} = \greenE{{?}}+\greenE{{?}}+\greenE{{?}}+\greenE{{?}}+\greenE{{?}}+\greenE{{?}}$

Since there are $\blueE{6}$ sides that are each $\greenD{?}\,\text{cm}$ long, we can also multiply.

$66\,\text{cm} = \blueE{6}\times\greenE{{?}}$
Each side of the model is $\greenE{11\,\text{cm}}$ long.
